We move forward as a democratic and welfare state by achieving economic prosperity through infrastructure development,\" Burlakoti told BBC Nepali."},{"@type":"BlogPosting","@id":"https://www.bbc.com/news/live/c93j7975n9et?post=asset:20af0250-116a-44b5-8884-d82a9ff5a5b1","isAccessibleForFree":"true","headline":"Court dispute holds results in one constituency","publisher":{"@type":"NewsMediaOrganization","name":"BBC News","url":"https://www.bbc.co.uk/news","logo":{"@type":"ImageObject","url":"https://static.files.bbci.co.uk/core/website/assets/static/news/images/metadata/poster-1024x576.5f404a8b8b.png"}},"author":{"@type":"NewsMediaOrganization","name":"BBC News","url":"https://www.bbc.co.uk/news","logo":{"@type":"ImageObject","url":"https://static.files.bbci.co.uk/core/website/assets/static/news/images/metadata/poster-1024x576.5f404a8b8b.png"}},"image":"https://ichef.bbci.co.uk/ace/standard/640/cpsprodpb/vivo/live/images/2026/3/9/820eed7f-6baf-4d0c-9823-923e8320e3a3.jpg.webp","datePublished":"2026-03-09T04:45:05.733Z","dateModified":"2026-03-09T04:46:13.000Z","mainEntityOfPage":"https://www.bbc.com/news/live/c93j7975n9et","articleBody":"The Election Commission of Nepal (ECN) has stopped counting in one constituency and will withhold results until the coutry's highest court decides on a dispute. Rastriya Swatantra Party candidate Kishori Saha was disqualified in Dhanusha-1 after it was found that he was blacklisted by the Credit Information Centre, the country's watchdog on credit scores. However, Saha still got votes as the ballots had been printed before the disqualification. The ECN said votes cast for Saha are void and now the case has been forwarded to the Supreme Court. The votes counted so far show that Saha was well in the lead. "},{"@type":"BlogPosting","@id":"https://www.bbc.com/news/live/c93j7975n9et?post=asset:3efcddb6-d52e-4eaa-91ad-2ce5cbf30759","isAccessibleForFree":"true","headline":"Nepal has a new government but no building for them to sit in","publisher":{"@type":"NewsMediaOrganization","name":"BBC News","url":"https://www.bbc.co.uk/news","logo":{"@type":"ImageObject","url":"https://static.files.bbci.co.uk/core/website/assets/static/news/images/metadata/poster-1024x576.5f404a8b8b.png"}},"author":{"@type":"NewsMediaOrganization","name":"BBC News","url":"https://www.bbc.co.uk/news","logo":{"@type":"ImageObject","url":"https://static.files.bbci.co.uk/core/website/assets/static/news/images/metadata/poster-1024x576.5f404a8b8b.png"}},"image":"https://ichef.bbci.co.uk/ace/standard/640/cpsprodpb/vivo/live/images/2026/3/9/5488769d-d1aa-49ea-b099-20caa004edbb.jpg.webp","datePublished":"2026-03-09T04:27:22.062Z","dateModified":"2026-03-09T04:27:21.000Z","mainEntityOfPage":"https://www.bbc.com/news/live/c93j7975n9et","articleBody":"The full election results will be official by tomorrow but one fundamental question confronts the 265 members of the new parliament - where will they hold office? The parliament building was set on fire at the height of Gen Z protests last year. However, construction on the new structure meant to replace it has not been completed. According to the Kathmandu Post, the Ministry of Urban Development has given assurances the building will be ready by mid-March. It was originally due to be completed last October."},{"@type":"BlogPosting","@id":"https://www.bbc.com/news/live/c93j7975n9et?post=asset:dcb6e140-58a5-4087-bf95-fbbb5773c8d2","isAccessibleForFree":"true","headline":"Election Commissioner on why the vote count was faster this year","publisher":{"@type":"NewsMediaOrganization","name":"BBC News","url":"https://www.bbc.co.uk/news","logo":{"@type":"ImageObject","url":"https://static.files.bbci.co.uk/core/website/assets/static/news/images/metadata/poster-1024x576.5f404a8b8b.png"}},"author":{"@type":"NewsMediaOrganization","name":"BBC News","url":"https://www.bbc.co.uk/news","logo":{"@type":"ImageObject","url":"https://static.files.bbci.co.uk/core/website/assets/static/news/images/metadata/poster-1024x576.5f404a8b8b.png"}},"image":"https://ichef.bbci.co.uk/ace/standard/640/cpsprodpb/vivo/live/images/2026/3/9/379ea176-cc31-47bd-af93-7b240631c2ff.jpg.webp","datePublished":"2026-03-09T04:11:28.797Z","dateModified":"2026-03-09T04:16:18.000Z","mainEntityOfPage":"https://www.bbc.com/news/live/c93j7975n9et","articleBody":"Better deployment of staff and \"good preparation\" are responsible for the speedier-than-usual vote count at this election, Nepal’s acting chief election commissioner says. Ram Prasad Bhandari told the BBC the commission had delegated authority to local election officers. \"Our management, deployment of staff, and good preparation are the reasons for the early results,\" he said. \"All the results will be out by tomorrow evening.\" During the last election in 2022, it took nearly two weeks for results to be released. Collecting ballot boxes can be challenging in Nepal, where more than 80% of the terrain is mountainous. As of Monday, the counting for the first-past-the-post system is almost complete, with only three constituencies remaining to be hold. However, the final declaration for the district of Dhanusa is on hold pending a court decision expected later on Monday. Bhandari also confirmed that 60% of the counting for the proportional representation system, which determines the final makeup of the 275-seat House of Representatives, was complete with the remaining ballots expected to be processed in the next 24 hours."},{"@type":"BlogPosting","@id":"https://www.bbc.com/news/live/c93j7975n9et?post=asset:115f797d-bc54-4f6b-8d34-a99b5f3780ab","isAccessibleForFree":"true","headline":"What's next for Nepal's old guard?","publisher":{"@type":"NewsMediaOrganization","name":"BBC News","url":"https://www.bbc.co.uk/news","logo":{"@type":"ImageObject","url":"https://static.files.bbci.co.uk/core/website/assets/static/news/images/metadata/poster-1024x576.5f404a8b8b.png"}},"author":{"@type":"NewsMediaOrganization","name":"BBC News","url":"https://www.bbc.co.uk/news","logo":{"@type":"ImageObject","url":"https://static.files.bbci.co.uk/core/website/assets/static/news/images/metadata/poster-1024x576.5f404a8b8b.png"}},"image":"https://ichef.bbci.co.uk/ace/standard/640/cpsprodpb/vivo/live/images/2026/3/9/a29707d4-b742-43e6-86d9-4c73676ced0c.jpg.webp","datePublished":"2026-03-09T03:54:55.011Z","dateModified":"2026-03-09T04:04:18.000Z","mainEntityOfPage":"https://www.bbc.com/news/live/c93j7975n9et","articleBody":"For Nepal's older parties, this verdict can bring absolute changes to their politics and organisational structures. But it will be difficult for them to regain the trust of the people, particularly the younger Nepalis. But Nepali parties have survived worse vicissitudes, not least the three-decade-long royal autocracy and a civil war. Here is a moment for the older parties to return to the roots of the ideologies and methods to introspect about what delivered them to power over and over again (and why they were rejected). "},{"@type":"BlogPosting","@id":"https://www.bbc.com/news/live/c93j7975n9et?post=asset:15714f4b-8549-4a93-bcd2-2cb433e0e9f8","isAccessibleForFree":"true","headline":"Old guard Prachanda withstands Balen wave","publisher":{"@type":"NewsMediaOrganization","name":"BBC News","url":"https://www.bbc.co.uk/news","logo":{"@type":"ImageObject","url":"https://static.files.bbci.co.uk/core/website/assets/static/news/images/metadata/poster-1024x576.5f404a8b8b.png"}},"author":{"@type":"NewsMediaOrganization","name":"BBC News","url":"https://www.bbc.co.uk/news","logo":{"@type":"ImageObject","url":"https://static.files.bbci.co.uk/core/website/assets/static/news/images/metadata/poster-1024x576.5f404a8b8b.png"}},"image":"https://ichef.bbci.co.uk/ace/standard/640/cpsprodpb/vivo/live/images/2026/3/9/e82a3c17-69d7-4a36-9696-9078c3b487c3.jpg.webp","datePublished":"2026-03-09T03:43:03.341Z","dateModified":"2026-03-09T03:43:02.000Z","mainEntityOfPage":"https://www.bbc.com/news/live/c93j7975n9et","articleBody":"There's one member of Nepal's old guard who won in the election - former prime minister Pushpa Kamal Dahal also known as Prachanda. Prachanda is a former Maoist leader who fought a bloody 10-year war against the monarchy and later transitioned to politics. His guerilla name Prachanda means Fierce One. Prachanda of the Nepali Communist Party won in Rukum East for his fifth consecutive term in parliament, local media report. Earlier, we reported that veteran politicians KP Sharma Oli and Gagan Thapa both lost to candidates from Balendra Shah's party. The trigger was a government ban on 26 social media platforms, but people who took to the streets expressed anger over bigger issues. Many felt the country’s succession of governments had failed to deliver on promises to address Nepal's longstanding economic problems. Others accused officials and members of the country’s social elite of corruption and nepotism. Within less than 48 hours, the Gen Z-led, digitally-driven anti-corruption protests had brought down the government. But their victory came at a heavy price. With 77 people killed, the protests were the deadliest unrest in the Himalayan country in decades. Official buildings, residences of political leaders and luxury hotels such as the Hilton, which opened in July 2024, were torched, vandalised and looted. In the aftermath, the protesters gave their approval to a new interim prime minister and demanded new elections. Now, six months later, they are getting their wish."}]},{"@context":"http://schema.org","@type":"WebPage","description":"The Rastriya Swatantra Party (RSP) is dominating the count, with Shah on course to be prime minister.","url":"https://www.bbc.com/news/live/c93j7975n9et","mainEntityOfPage":"https://www.bbc.com/news/live/c93j7975n9et","publisher":{"@type":"NewsMediaOrganization","name":"BBC News","url":"https://www.bbc.co.uk/news","logo":{"@type":"ImageObject","url":"https://static.files.bbci.co.uk/core/website/assets/static/news/images/metadata/poster-1024x576.5f404a8b8b.png"}},"name":"Nepal election 2026 results - follow live"},{"@context":"http://schema.org","@type":"NewsArticle","dateModified":"2026-03-10T12:00:00.000Z","datePublished":"2026-03-09T00:20:42.372Z","description":"The Rastriya Swatantra Party (RSP) is dominating the count, with Shah on course to be prime minister.","headline":"Nepal election 2026 results - follow live","image":{"@type":"ImageObject","width":{"@type":"QuantitativeValue","unitText":"px","value":1024},"height":{"@type":"QuantitativeValue","unitText":"px","value":576},"url":"https://ichef.bbci.co.uk/ace/standard/1024/cpsprodpb/a8b3/live/109f32e0-1b48-11f1-a216-1d27169d9c1c.jpg"},"author":{"@type":"NewsMediaOrganization","name":"BBC News","url":"https://www.bbc.co.uk/news","logo":{"@type":"ImageObject","url":"https://static.files.bbci.co.uk/core/website/assets/static/news/images/metadata/poster-1024x576.5f404a8b8b.png"}},"publisher":{"@type":"NewsMediaOrganization","name":"BBC News","url":"https://www.bbc.co.uk/news","logo":{"@type":"ImageObject","url":"https://static.files.bbci.co.uk/core/website/assets/static/news/images/metadata/poster-1024x576.5f404a8b8b.png"}},"mainEntityOfPage":"https://www.bbc.com/news/live/c93j7975n9et"}]}

How close is the RSP to a two-thirds majority? published at 06:53 GMT 06:53 GMT Of the 275 parliamentary seats up for grabs in this election, 184 are required to secure a two-thirds majority. Balendra Shah's RSP has won 124 of the 165 seats decided by direct voting, as well as 58 of the 110 seats allocated through proportional representation. This means the party is now just two seats short of that threshold. If it does manage the feat, this will mark the first time in decades that a single party has done that. Nepal's dual voting system means it is difficult for any one party to win a simple majority, let alone a two-thirds one. Share close panel Share page Copy link About sharing Balen Shah's party defeats old guard by a landslide published at 06:23 GMT 06:23 GMT Image source, Getty Images The party of rapper-turned-mayor Balendra Shah has crushed Nepal's old guard in parliamentary election. The RSP's landlside victory shows that the issues raised by Gen Z in last year's protests - corruption, inequality and a revolving door of elite rules - has resonated across generations, analysts say. Here is what you need to know: Shah's Rastriya Swatantra Party is on track to win a two-thirds majority, positioning the ex-mayor of Kathmandu to be the country's next prime minister

Shah has also defeated former prime minister KP Sharma Oli in Japha 5 constituency while another RSP candidate beat Nepali Congress president, Gagan Thapa, in Sarlahi 4

There's one member of Nepal's old guard who managed to win their seat - Maoist leader-turned-prime minister Pushpa Kamal Dahal also known as Prachanda

It's a seismic moment in Nepali politics that gives new hope for youth protesters who marched to Kathmandu last September, writes our South Asia correspondent Azadeh Moshiri

Shah's supporters have taken to the streets to celebrate, ringing bells - the RSP's election symbol

For older politicians, the rejection by voters is a chance to return to the roots of their ideologies and think about what delivered them to power before, writes Kathmandu-based analyst Amish Raj Mulmi

Full results are expected soon. However, there is one problem - the new parliament building is unfinished and the old one was damaged by fire during the protests Share close panel Share page Copy link About sharing What's next for Nepal's new leaders? published at 06:02 GMT 06:02 GMT Amish Raj Mulmi

Analyst based in Kathmandu The sweeping verdict translates into high expectations from the incoming government. The Nepali people – particularly the youth – are exhausted. Reforms are essential, both political and economic. A majority means the RSP will be able to translate their promises into policy, but the absence of a strong opposition means vigilance will also be required on the methods and goals of such reforms. There is also great pressure to release the Karki Commission’s report on the September protests and prosecute those guilty for the killings on 8 September, as well as to begin investigating the old political class for corruption. The immediate challenge will be the US-Israel war with Iran, the safety of the large number of Nepali diaspora in West Asia, and managing the economic fallout of the war. Share close panel Share page Copy link About sharing A seismic shock for Nepal's old guard published at 05:47 GMT 05:47 GMT Azadeh Moshiri

South Asia Correspondent The numbers so far are already a seismic shock, in a country used to the same familiar faces exchanging power for decades. It’s given hope to protesters who marched for an end to corruption last September. Nandan, 18, was one of them, and voted for the first time. He told me this result has proven that Gen-Z voters like him are now the decision makers. Balen, as he’s known, is set to become Nepal's youngest ever prime minister, leading a party that is only four years old. He has already beaten Nepal’s former leader KP Sharma Oli, a veteran politician, who was ousted by protesters. Voters have signalled they are ready for an end to the fragile coalitions that have dominated politics in Nepal, and the beginning of a new political era. Share close panel Share page Copy link About sharing What are the defeated old parties saying? published at 05:25 GMT 05:25 GMT The results are "unexpected", says Pradeep Gyawali from the CPN-UML, the party of former prime minister KP Sharma Oli. Balen Shah beat the former prime minister in his traditional stronghold Japha 5. "Right now, we only wish that democracy is strengthened, and that there are no compromises on national interests, and that no decisions are made on impulse at a time when geopolitical situations are becoming sensitive," Gyawali says. Nepali Communist Party leader Agni Sapkot says the initial results are "thought-provoking". The party's senior leader Prachanda, another former PM, was one of the only members of the old guard to withstand the Balen Shah wave, defeating the RSP candidate in his constituency. "The election has taken place, the people have voted. Don't underestimate it now," Sapkota added. Congress Vice President Bishwa Prakash Sharma said: "We must move forward by accepting the results that Nepali voters have given us". Congress' PM bet, Gagan Thapa lost to the RSP candidate like Oli. Share close panel Share page Copy link About sharing Balen Shah's party promises good governance published at 05:02 GMT 05:02 GMT Image source, Getty Images The RSP has reiterated its commitment to good governance with its landslide election victory all but certain. The party's General Secretary, Kavindra Burlakoti, has issued a statement with counting near ccompleted, with official results out as early as tomorrow. "We move towards prosperity with the daily work of service delivery. We move forward as a democratic and welfare state by achieving economic prosperity through infrastructure development," Burlakoti told BBC Nepali. Share close panel Share page Copy link About sharing Court dispute holds results in one constituency published at 04:45 GMT 04:45 GMT Image source, Getty Images The Election Commission of Nepal (ECN) has stopped counting in one constituency and will withhold results until the coutry's highest court decides on a dispute. Rastriya Swatantra Party candidate Kishori Saha was disqualified in Dhanusha-1 after it was found that he was blacklisted by the Credit Information Centre, the country's watchdog on credit scores. However, Saha still got votes as the ballots had been printed before the disqualification. The ECN said votes cast for Saha are void and now the case has been forwarded to the Supreme Court. The votes counted so far show that Saha was well in the lead. Share close panel Share page Copy link About sharing Nepal has a new government but no building for them to sit in published at 04:27 GMT 04:27 GMT Image source, Getty Images The full election results will be official by tomorrow but one fundamental question confronts the 265 members of the new parliament - where will they hold office? The parliament building was set on fire at the height of Gen Z protests last year. However, construction on the new structure meant to replace it has not been completed. According to the Kathmandu Post, the Ministry of Urban Development has given assurances the building will be ready by mid-March. It was originally due to be completed last October. Image source, Getty Images Share close panel Share page Copy link About sharing Election Commissioner on why the vote count was faster this year published at 04:11 GMT 04:11 GMT Rajneesh Bhandari

BBC Better deployment of staff and "good preparation" are responsible for the speedier-than-usual vote count at this election, Nepal’s acting chief election commissioner says. Ram Prasad Bhandari told the BBC the commission had delegated authority to local election officers. "Our management, deployment of staff, and good preparation are the reasons for the early results," he said. "All the results will be out by tomorrow evening." During the last election in 2022, it took nearly two weeks for results to be released. Collecting ballot boxes can be challenging in Nepal, where more than 80% of the terrain is mountainous. As of Monday, the counting for the first-past-the-post system is almost complete, with only three constituencies remaining to be hold. However, the final declaration for the district of Dhanusa is on hold pending a court decision expected later on Monday. Bhandari also confirmed that 60% of the counting for the proportional representation system, which determines the final makeup of the 275-seat House of Representatives, was complete with the remaining ballots expected to be processed in the next 24 hours. Image source, Rajneesh Bhandari Image caption, Vote counting in the city of Lalitpur on Monday morning Share close panel Share page Copy link About sharing What's next for Nepal's old guard? published at 03:54 GMT 03:54 GMT Amish Raj Mulmi

analyst based in Kathmandu Image caption, Former PM KP Sharma Oli has lost the Jhapa 5 seat to Balendra Shah For Nepal's older parties, this verdict can bring absolute changes to their politics and organisational structures. But it will be difficult for them to regain the trust of the people, particularly the younger Nepalis. But Nepali parties have survived worse vicissitudes, not least the three-decade-long royal autocracy and a civil war. Here is a moment for the older parties to return to the roots of the ideologies and methods to introspect about what delivered them to power over and over again (and why they were rejected). Share close panel Share page Copy link About sharing Old guard Prachanda withstands Balen wave published at 03:43 GMT 03:43 GMT Image source, Reuters There's one member of Nepal's old guard who won in the election - former prime minister Pushpa Kamal Dahal also known as Prachanda. Prachanda is a former Maoist leader who fought a bloody 10-year war against the monarchy and later transitioned to politics. His guerilla name Prachanda means Fierce One. Prachanda of the Nepali Communist Party won in Rukum East for his fifth consecutive term in parliament, local media report. Earlier, we reported that veteran politicians KP Sharma Oli and Gagan Thapa both lost to candidates from Balendra Shah's party. Singh is a member of Balendra Shah's Rastriya Swatantra Party. Thapa is from the country's largest party, the Nepali Congress. He suffered the same electoral fate as former prime minister KP Sharma Oli, who lost to Shah in Japha 5. Six other Nepali Congress leaders have also lost their seats, local media say. Share close panel Share page Copy link About sharing What's next for Oli after election defeat? published at 03:09 GMT 03:09 GMT After being handed a crushing defeat by voters, former prime minister KP Sharma Oli's Communist Party of Nepal-Unified Marxist Leninist (CPN-UML) will hold discuss a possible re-organisation and leadership change. Oli has lost against Balen Shah in his traditional stronghold Japha 5 and Shah's Rastriya Swatantra Party (RSP) is on course to get a a two-thirds majority in parliament. It's a "serious setback", CPN-UML leaders say. "We need to move forward in a new way, free ourselves from traditional ways, and move forward by humbly criticizing ourselves for past mistakes and going among the people," CPN-UML President Raghuji Pant told local media. Share close panel Share page Copy link About sharing Gen Z voter wants local opportunities for students published at 02:51 GMT 02:51 GMT Rajneesh Bhandari

BBC As a 20-year-old first-time voter, Tamanna Kumari Rana is optimistic following Balendra Shah's win, saying she believes his leadership will drive national development. Tamanna, who was on her way to her college on Monday morning when she spoke to the BBC, says the new government should focus on the education system and creating local opportunities so that students aren't forced to move abroad for work or study. “There should be no corruption. I hope the roads will be built better. There will be water and electricity as well,” she says. Share close panel Share page Copy link About sharing Nepal seems set to break from its past published at 02:34 GMT 02:34 GMT Azadeh Moshiri

South Asia Correspondent Image source, Reuters The result so far gives hope to Nepalis who want a break from the past. A government led by Balen, as he’s known, will end decades of fragile coalitions that have dominated politics in Nepal. Balen chose to run in the same constituency, Jhapa 5, as KP Sharma Oli, the four-time former prime minister forced from office by the youth-led anti-corruption protests. For years Jhapa 5 had been a stronghold for Oli, but newcomer Balen appears to be winning by a landslide, and he is on course to become Nepal's youngest ever prime minister. His party itself is less than four years old. Oli told us he was “confident” he and his party would win. The results so far will shake his belief that the protests were not a referendum on his leadership. These partial results seem to signal a chance for a new, untried, generation to shape the future. Share close panel Share page Copy link About sharing The Balen Shah Wave published at 02:14 GMT 02:14 GMT Amish Raj Mulmi

Analyst based in Kathmandu Image source, Reuters Balen’s entry into national politics was a turning point for the RSP. Projected to be the youngest prime minister in the history of democratic Nepal, Balen will also be the country's first Madhesi prime minister. The Madhes movement has been a critical feature for the long struggle for rights in Nepal, and his candidacy was seen as a moment to install "a son of the Madhes soil" in Nepal’s most important position. His popularity was such that in several constituencies, people voted for the RSP without knowing who their local RSP candidate was – thus turning the campaign into a presidential style campaign which the older parties had no answer to. The quest for political stability through an electoral majority – which was one of the campaign lines for the RSP – has come to fruition, something most had thought would not be possible under the current electoral system. Share close panel Share page Copy link About sharing This young voter wants the new government to create more jobs published at 01:57 GMT 01:57 GMT Rajneesh Bhandari

BBC As an 18-year-old first-time voter, David Paudel expresses satisfaction with the results. He believes the new government should break away from past patterns and focus on effective governance. David says he participated in the Gen Z movement in September last year, advocating for the fight against corruption. The new government, David says, should focus on creating more jobs in the country. “Things like employment for the youth in the country, and issues of development—they (the new government) should work on those important issues,” he says. Share close panel Share page Copy link About sharing In pics: Balen Shah's supporters celebrate in Nepal published at 01:39 GMT 01:39 GMT Supporters of Balen Shah have taken to the streets to cheer the historic win of his Rastriya Swatantra Party (RSP). Final results are not yet out, but it is clear that the RSP have won a direct majority. Some are ringing bells - the RSP's symbol - in celebration. Image source, Getty Images Image source, Getty Images Image source, Getty Images Image source, Getty Images Image source, Getty Images Share close panel Share page Copy link About sharing “Hope for a new political culture,” says Gen-Z voter published at 01:26 GMT 01:26 GMT Azadeh Moshiri

South Asia Correspondent The last time I met Ispa, she was waving an RSP flag with her friends at a campaign rally in Jhapa 5. She protested in September and told us Balen represented the change Gen-Z marched for. Now, she says the result in Oli’s former stronghold of Jhapa 5 is evidence that Balen appealed to older Nepalis too. “The fact that he gained strong support even in Jhapa 5 shows that people across the country are looking for new leadership,” she says. “He talks about transparency, accountability, and real change in politics.” Ipsa feels the result has shaken Nepal’s politics. “Balen represents hope for a new political culture in Nepal.” Share close panel Share page Copy link About sharing What happened in last year’s protests? published at 01:16 GMT 01:16 GMT Image source, Getty Images In September 2025, thousands of Nepalis heeded a call by demonstrators describing themselves as Generation Z to gather near the parliament building in the capital Kathmandu. The trigger was a government ban on 26 social media platforms, but people who took to the streets expressed anger over bigger issues. Many felt the country’s succession of governments had failed to deliver on promises to address Nepal's longstanding economic problems. Others accused officials and members of the country’s social elite of corruption and nepotism. Within less than 48 hours, the Gen Z-led, digitally-driven anti-corruption protests had brought down the government. But their victory came at a heavy price. With 77 people killed, the protests were the deadliest unrest in the Himalayan country in decades. Official buildings, residences of political leaders and luxury hotels such as the Hilton, which opened in July 2024, were torched, vandalised and looted. In the aftermath, the protesters gave their approval to a new interim prime minister and demanded new elections. Now, six months later, they are getting their wish.
